title: Milk on Cotton
timeEstimate: 15–30 minutes for fresh washable cotton; older or dried milk may need professional review
stainChemistry: Milk/dairy is treated as a protein stain with possible fat and sugar residue. The supported Home rule is cold/cool first: remove excess, rinse or soak with cold/cool water if washable, use generic liquid laundry detergent or care-label-safe enzyme detergent, then launder by the care label and air-dry before heat. This card does not make public-health, disinfection, or hot-wash odor-removal claims.
whyThisWorks: Cold/cool water helps remove fresh protein before heat can set it. Detergent helps loosen residues, and enzyme-containing detergent may help protein stains on washable cotton when the care label and product label allow it. Air-drying before the dryer prevents heat from locking in remaining protein or odor.
defaultAssumption: Use only for washable cotton. Dry-clean-only, structured, embellished, vintage, high-value, unstable-dye, old/dried/yellowed, or odor-persistent milk items should escalate instead of being heat-treated at home.

spottingProtocol.technique: Care-label, construction, fiber, and colorfastness assessment
spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean white cloth, hidden seam/hem test
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Stop immediately on transfer or change
spottingProtocol.instruction: Confirm cotton is safe for wet treatment and document any Home attempts. Test colorfastness on a hidden seam or hem before any spotting.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Pro-only intake. Follow care label; reject or escalate unstable dye, structured construction, or high-value textiles.

spottingProtocol.technique: Use the mildest source-supported professional path first
spottingProtocol.equipment: Product-labeled detergent or family-specific spotter; clean towels
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Use product-label dwell only; stop on dye movement or texture change
spottingProtocol.instruction: Treat milk on cotton as a protein/dairy stain and begin with controlled detergent/water action before any stronger chemistry. Keep chemistry bounded by SDS/label and fabric test results.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Pro-only. Use PPE/ventilation required by product SDS; do not expose Home users to pro solvents, acids, oxidizers, or concentrated spotters.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush between products and inspect dry
spottingProtocol.equipment: Water/steam only if fabric and product label permit; drying rack
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Until residues are flushed and fabric is dry enough to inspect
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush or rinse out chemistry before switching products. Air-dry/inspect before considering any heat, oxidizer, solvent, or additional professional escalation.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Pro-only. Do not mix incompatible chemistry; use PPE/ventilation and document any last-resort escalation.

products: Product-labeled professional protein/detergent systems only, used per SDS/label
professionalProtocol.temperature: Cold/cool first; any warmer professional step only after testing, label review, and protein-path control
warnings: Pro-only protein digesters or stronger chemistry require SDS/label controls, PPE, ventilation, and flushing. No hot water on protein/dairy before removal.

homeSolutions.instruction: Check the care label. If the cotton is dry-clean-only, structured, embellished, vintage, high-value, or dye-unstable, stop and take it to a professional. For washable cotton, keep the treatment cold or cool at the start.
Blot liquid milk with a clean white cloth or gently lift any thicker residue. Do not rub; rubbing drives protein deeper into the cotton.
Rinse from the back with cold or cool water, or soak briefly in cold/cool water if the care label allows. Do not use hot water while milk protein may remain.

homeSolutions.agent: Generic Detergent or Care-Label-Safe Enzyme Detergent
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply a small amount of generic liquid laundry detergent. If the garment and product label allow enzyme detergent, use it as directed. Test colored cotton on a hidden seam first and stop if dye transfers.
Wash according to the care label using cool or care-label-safe water. Air-dry completely and inspect before any dryer or iron.

If it is improving, repeat the conservative cold/cool detergent path once. Escalate if odor remains after air-drying, yellowing appears, dye bleeds, the milk was old/dried, or there is no improvement after two rounds.

steps: 1. Care Label and Cold/Cool Start: Check the care label. If the cotton is dry-clean-only, structured, embellished, vintage, high-value, or dye-unstable, stop and take it to a professional. For washable cotton, keep the treatment cold or cool at the start.
2. Blot or Lift Excess Milk: Blot liquid milk with a clean white cloth or gently lift any thicker residue. Do not rub; rubbing drives protein deeper into the cotton.
3. Cold/Cool Rinse or Short Soak: Rinse from the back with cold or cool water, or soak briefly in cold/cool water if the care label allows. Do not use hot water while milk protein may remain.

4. Generic Detergent or Care-Label-Safe Enzyme Detergent: Apply a small amount of generic liquid laundry detergent. If the garment and product label allow enzyme detergent, use it as directed. Test colored cotton on a hidden seam first and stop if dye transfers.
5. Care-Label Wash, Then Air-Dry: Wash according to the care label using cool or care-label-safe water. Air-dry completely and inspect before any dryer or iron.

6. Repeat Once or Escalate: If it is improving, repeat the conservative cold/cool detergent path once. Escalate if odor remains after air-drying, yellowing appears, dye bleeds, the milk was old/dried, or there is no improvement after two rounds.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Escalate old/dried milk, odor after air-drying, yellowing, dye bleed, dry-clean-only/structured/valuable items, or no improvement after two conservative rounds.
warnings: No hot water, dryer, iron, or heat until the protein path is complete and the stain is gone
No Home peroxide, acids, solvents, or pro protein concentrates

neverDo: Never put the item in a dryer, use an iron, or apply heat until the stain is gone after air-drying inspection
Never rub or scrub aggressively; blot, lift, or gently work only enough to avoid driving soil deeper
Never mix cleaning agents, bleach, ammonia, acids, solvents, or professional spotters
Never use chlorine bleach unless the care label allows it and the fabric is white or confirmed colorfast
Never use professional-strength oxidizers, acids, dry-side solvents, rust/mineral removers, adhesive removers, or named pro products in the Home path
Never use hot water on milk/dairy protein before the protein stain is removed
Never promise public-health, sanitizing, or food-safety disinfection results from a stain-removal wash
fiberSensitivities: Cotton is cellulose; washable cotton can generally tolerate detergent and care-label-safe enzyme detergent
Colored cotton needs lower-temperature caution and a hidden white-cloth colorfastness test before detergent dwell or escalation
chemicalBoundaries: Home path is limited to water, generic liquid laundry detergent or heavy-duty liquid laundry detergent, and care-label laundering
Use any detergent, enzyme detergent, or pretreat only according to its product label and only after a hidden-area test on colored fabric
Oxidizers are not routine Home steps; any trace-color escalation must be care-label-safe, white/colorfast, hidden-test-passed, and never mixed with other cleaners
Professional chemistry is Pro-only and requires SDS/label controls, PPE, ventilation, colorfastness testing, and flush/rinse controls
Enzyme detergent is Home-allowed only when the cotton care label and product label allow it; do not generalize enzymes to silk, wool, or protein fibers
No peroxide, concentrated acids, chlorine bleach, or professional protein concentrates in the Home path
chemicalControls: Home path is limited to water, generic liquid laundry detergent or heavy-duty liquid laundry detergent, and care-label laundering
Use any detergent, enzyme detergent, or pretreat only according to its product label and only after a hidden-area test on colored fabric
Oxidizers are not routine Home steps; any trace-color escalation must be care-label-safe, white/colorfast, hidden-test-passed, and never mixed with other cleaners
Professional chemistry is Pro-only and requires SDS/label controls, PPE, ventilation, colorfastness testing, and flush/rinse controls
Enzyme detergent is Home-allowed only when the cotton care label and product label allow it; do not generalize enzymes to silk, wool, or protein fibers
No peroxide, concentrated acids, chlorine bleach, or professional protein concentrates in the Home path
homeAllowed: Cool/cold water or care-label-safe lukewarm water
Generic liquid laundry detergent or heavy-duty liquid laundry detergent
proOnly: Professional spotters, dry-side solvents, acids, oxidizers, reducers, concentrated chemistry, steam, and heat escalation require SDS/label controls, PPE, ventilation, flush/rinse controls, and documented fabric testing
testFirst: Test detergent, warmer water, enzyme detergent, oxidizer escalation, alcohol/solvent, acid spotter, or any pro chemistry on a hidden seam/hem with a clean white cloth before treating the visible area
safetyMatrix.colorfastnessTest: On a hidden seam or hem, press a clean white cloth with the planned detergent/water mix for one minute; stop if dye transfers, color changes, or texture changes.
safetyMatrix.flushNeutralizeDry: Rinse or flush out each cleaning agent before changing products; air-dry fully and inspect before any dryer, iron, heat, or professional escalation.
safetyMatrix.temperatureLimits: Cold/cool first for milk/dairy protein. No hot water, dryer, iron, or heat until the protein path is complete and the stain is gone.
escalationTriggers: Stop and escalate on dye bleed, color loss, texture change, ring formation, spreading halo, or fabric distortion
Stop and escalate dry-clean-only, non-washable, lined, structured, embellished, vintage, high-value, or unstable-dye items
Escalate old or dried milk, odor after air-drying, yellowing, dye bleed, or no improvement after two conservative rounds
stopAndEscalate: Stop and escalate on dye bleed, color loss, texture change, ring formation, spreading halo, or fabric distortion
Stop and escalate dry-clean-only, non-washable, lined, structured, embellished, vintage, high-value, or unstable-dye items
Escalate old or dried milk, odor after air-drying, yellowing, dye bleed, or no improvement after two conservative rounds
safetyMatrix.ppeVentilation: For Home detergent use, follow the product label and keep products away from children and pets. For concentrates or Pro chemistry, use gloves/eye protection as the SDS requires and work with ventilation.
safetyMatrix.homeVsProBoundary: Home guidance excludes professional-strength oxidizers, acids, solvents, named pro products, exact pro concentrations, steam, and heat escalation; those belong only in a professional workflow with SDS/label controls.

materialWarnings: Milk/dairy is protein: cold/cool first, no heat until gone
Colored cotton needs a hidden-area white-cloth dye-transfer test
Residual odor after air-drying is an escalation trigger, not a reason for Home hot-wash guarantees

escalation.when: Escalate old/dried milk, odor after air-drying, yellowing, dye bleed, dry-clean-only/structured/valuable items, or no improvement after two conservative rounds.
escalation.whatToTell: Milk/dairy on cotton; age of stain; whether cold/cool rinse, detergent, or enzyme detergent was used; whether odor, yellowing, or dye transfer remains after air-drying.
escalation.specialistType: professional textile cleaner or dry cleaner

contextQuestions: How old is the milk spill?
Has it been through hot water, a dryer, or an iron?
Is the cotton colored, white, structured, or dry-clean-only?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Keep dairy protein cold/cool until the protein path is complete; do not add sanitation claims.

sources.title: University Stain Science (compiled extension reference)
sources.evidence: Consumer stain-family guidance for protein, tannin, oil, combination and unknown stains; blot rather than rub, work from the wrong side where appropriate, test first, and do not iron or dry until the stain is removed.

sources.title: EPA Alternative Cleaning Curriculum
sources.evidence: Safety and process gates: heat, concentration, oxidation, abrasion and mismanaged water can damage textiles; detergents/surfactants suspend oily soils; test hidden areas, follow product labels, ventilate, use PPE for concentrates, avoid incompatible mixes, and clean only according to care label or reject/escalate.

sources.title: GONR Material Safety Matrix v2
sources.evidence: Internal cotton/linen safety boundaries; protein, oil, tannin and oxidizer controls; dry-side solvent/pro chemistry boundaries; no hot water on protein stains; never mix chlorine bleach with ammonia or acids.

sources.title: R.R. Street 4Streets On-the-Spot
sources.evidence: Professional classification and operator workflow context for food/beverage, oil, tannin, protein and combination stains; pro-only product taxonomy.

sources.title: BÜFA Professional Spotting Guide
sources.evidence: Professional training support for heat-sensitive food/sugar residues, protein coagulation/enzymes, and wine/fruit tannin classification; low suitability for Home copy.
